title: Severe Alcohol Withdrawal with Delirium Tremens
subtitle: 54 y/o male, hospital day 3 post-op, sudden agitation and hallucinations
nursesNote: 0245 - 54 y/o male, post-op day 3 from open cholecystectomy, called out by night nurse for acute change in behavior. PMH: HTN, hyperlipidemia, history of heavy alcohol use (per family, 'a fifth of vodka daily for 20 years'); patient initially denied use on admission and was not started on a withdrawal protocol. POD#1 he was tremulous and diaphoretic but attributed to post-op pain; POD#2 mildly anxious and slept poorly. Tonight: pulled out his IV, screaming about 'spiders crawling on the wall,' striking out at staff, oriented only to person, drenched in sweat, severe whole-body tremor. Last drink approximately 72 hours ago per family. Wife at bedside, frightened. CIWA-Ar score on initial nurse assessment: 28 (severe). Rapid response activated. ICU bed requested. New IV access being established; safety measures in place; chemistry, magnesium, and CBC drawn.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Identify delirium tremens vs other delirium
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish delirium tremens (DTs) rather than other causes of post-op delirium.

opts.text: Onset 72 hours after last drink with progressive autonomic hyperactivity (HR 102 -> 138, BP 148/92 -> 184/108) and tremor
opts.rat: DT timeline is diagnostic. Withdrawal symptoms 6-12 hr; seizures peak 24-48 hr; DTs peak 48-96 hr. The 72-hour mark + escalating autonomic instability fits perfectly. Other deliriums (UTI, sepsis, hypoxia) don't follow this pattern.

opts.text: Visual hallucinations (spiders on walls), severe agitation with combativeness, disorientation, and severe diaphoresis - all key components of DTs
opts.rat: DSM-5 DT criteria: alcohol withdrawal + delirium (disturbance in attention/awareness) + cognitive disturbance + perceptual disturbances. Visual hallucinations are classic; tactile hallucinations (formication - feeling bugs crawling) also common. CIWA-Ar 28 = severe.

opts.text: Documented heavy chronic alcohol use (~750 mL vodka/day for 20 years) with last drink 72 hours ago, NO ethanol on labs, no other substances
opts.rat: Confirms the trigger. Not all post-op delirium is DTs - the history is critical. Negative ethanol confirms he hasn't been drinking; negative drug screen rules out other withdrawal/intoxication. Pattern locked in.

opts.text: Magnesium 1.2 and phosphorus 1.6
opts.rat: Common in chronic alcoholism (poor nutrition + renal wasting) and contribute to seizure risk - but they don't ESTABLISH DTs. Important to correct as part of treatment, not a diagnostic cue.

opts.text: AST 186 / ALT 94 with AST:ALT ratio >2
opts.rat: AST:ALT >2 is supportive of alcoholic liver disease (the classic 'AST is greater than ALT in Alcoholism' mnemonic) - confirms history of heavy use but doesn't establish acute DTs.

opts.text: Glucose 78 mg/dL
opts.rat: Borderline-low but not hypoglycemic. Normal finding; rules OUT hypoglycemia as cause of AMS, but isn't itself a DT cue.

opts.text: Recent abdominal surgery
opts.rat: Risk factor for post-op delirium broadly - but doesn't distinguish DTs from other post-op deliriums (anesthesia, infection, hypoxia, electrolytes). The history of alcohol use is the differentiator.

opts.text: Ammonia 42 mcg/dL
opts.rat: Normal - rules OUT hepatic encephalopathy as the AMS cause. Useful to exclude alternatives but not a DT cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Chronic alcohol use causes neuroadaptation: GABA receptor downregulation and NMDA receptor upregulation. When alcohol is suddenly removed, the brain has reduced inhibition AND amplified excitation - causing autonomic surge, seizures, and DTs
opts.rat: Core neurochemistry of withdrawal. Explains why benzodiazepines (GABA agonists) are first-line treatment - they replace the inhibitory tone that alcohol was providing while the brain re-equilibrates.

opts.text: Thiamine MUST be administered BEFORE any glucose-containing fluids in chronic alcoholics to prevent precipitating Wernicke's encephalopathy or Korsakoff syndrome
opts.rat: The 'banana bag' rule. Alcoholics are thiamine-depleted; carbohydrate metabolism uses thiamine; giving glucose first depletes the last reserves and triggers Wernicke's (ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, confusion - which can become permanent Korsakoff amnesia). Standard order: thiamine 100 mg IV, then folate, then glucose.

opts.text: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing using CIWA-Ar (e.g., lorazepam 2-4 mg IV q15min until calmed) is preferred over fixed-dose schedules - it treats the patient's actual state, uses less total medication, and shortens treatment duration
opts.rat: Evidence-based standard. Front-loading with CIWA-Ar guidance reduces total benzo exposure, length of stay, and complications vs fixed q4-q6 dosing. Lorazepam preferred in liver disease (no active metabolites). Diazepam is alternative with longer half-life.

opts.text: Magnesium and phosphorus replacement are unnecessary unless levels drop below 1.0 mg/dL
opts.rat: WRONG. Both should be replaced aggressively in alcohol withdrawal. Hypomagnesemia lowers seizure threshold and causes refractory arrhythmias; hypophosphatemia causes muscle weakness and rhabdomyolysis. Replace early.

opts.text: Haloperidol is the first-line treatment for the agitation and hallucinations of DTs
opts.rat: WRONG and DANGEROUS. Antipsychotics LOWER seizure threshold in withdrawal and don't treat the underlying GABAergic deficiency. Reserved for psychotic features unresponsive to adequate benzodiazepine - and even then second-generation agents are preferred. NCLEX trap.

opts.text: Beta-blockers control the autonomic symptoms (tachycardia, hypertension) and treat DTs definitively
opts.rat: WRONG. Beta-blockers MASK autonomic warning signs without treating underlying excitatory toxicity. They can give false reassurance while seizures still develop. Not appropriate as primary or sole therapy.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the diagnoses and contributors from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Delirium tremens (severe alcohol withdrawal) on POD#3 in an unrecognized chronic heavy drinker
opts.rat: Timeline, autonomic surge, hallucinations, agitation, CIWA 28, history of heavy use, negative substances. Clinically diagnostic.

opts.text: ICU/post-op delirium with multiple contributors (sleep deprivation, pain medications, electrolyte derangements)
opts.rat: Important differential - all post-op delirium causes can coexist. The alcohol history makes DTs primary; other contributors should still be addressed.

opts.text: Post-operative sepsis or pneumonia causing delirium
opts.rat: Worth ruling out - infection is a leading cause of post-op delirium. The classic DT pattern argues against sepsis as primary, but cultures and CXR are part of the workup.

opts.text: Wernicke's encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency)
opts.rat: Possible coexisting condition in any alcoholic - classic triad of confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia. Lower probability acutely but treat empirically with thiamine because consequences of missing it are severe (permanent Korsakoff).

opts.text: Hepatic encephalopathy from alcoholic liver disease
opts.rat: Ammonia is normal - rules out. Reasonable to consider given LFT pattern but unlikely to be the current driver.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30 minutes.

opts.text: Administer IV lorazepam 2-4 mg q15min titrated to CIWA-Ar score until patient is calm but arousable; transfer to ICU for continuous monitoring
opts.rat: Front-loading benzodiazepine is the intervention that prevents seizures and treats the underlying excitotoxicity. ICU monitoring needed for severe DTs (CIWA >20). Some patients need 100+ mg lorazepam in first 24 hr - dose to symptoms, not predetermined ceiling.

opts.text: Administer IV thiamine 100 mg BEFORE any dextrose-containing fluids; follow with multivitamin (folate, B-complex) and magnesium 2 g IV
opts.rat: The 'thiamine-before-glucose' rule prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy precipitation. Magnesium replacement reduces seizure risk and is often profoundly low in alcoholics. Standard 'banana bag' care.

opts.text: Implement safety measures: 1:1 sit-with-patient observation, padded side rails up, bed in lowest position, remove tubing/cords from reach, ensure suction and oxygen at bedside, prepare for possible seizure
opts.rat: DTs carry 5-15% mortality, often from injury during agitation, aspiration during seizure, or autonomic cardiovascular collapse. 1:1 observation is the standard - chemical sedation alone without environmental safety has caused deaths.

opts.text: Administer IV haloperidol 5 mg for the hallucinations and agitation
opts.rat: Lowers seizure threshold and doesn't treat underlying GABA deficiency. Common error to use it for the agitation/hallucinations - benzodiazepines treat both AND prevent seizures.

opts.text: Apply 4-point physical restraints as the primary intervention for combativeness
opts.rat: Restraints are LAST resort, not first-line. Increase agitation, autonomic activation, rhabdomyolysis risk, and aspiration. Adequate chemical sedation usually obviates need; if used, must be with continuous observation and frequent assessment per CMS standards.

opts.text: Initiate IV beta-blocker (metoprolol) to control HR 138 and BP 184/108
opts.rat: Masks autonomic warning signs without treating cause. The HR and BP will normalize as benzo loading achieves control. Beta-blockers can lead to false reassurance and undertreatment.

opts.text: Administer IV phenytoin loading dose to prevent withdrawal seizures
opts.rat: Phenytoin does NOT prevent alcohol withdrawal seizures - those are GABA-mediated, not focal. Benzodiazepines prevent withdrawal seizures. Phenytoin only useful if patient has independent seizure disorder.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Establish 1:1 observation, secure new IV access (large bore), apply continuous cardiac and SpO2 monitoring, ensure suction and oxygen at bedside, side rails padded
opts.rat: FIRST - safety setup before any medication. Patient already pulled out previous IV; new access is required for any treatment. Environmental safety prevents injury during agitation and seizure.

opts.text: Administer IV thiamine 100 mg, then magnesium sulfate 2 g IV, then begin lorazepam 2-4 mg IV with q15min CIWA-Ar reassessment
opts.rat: SECOND - thiamine FIRST (the classic NCLEX safety order), then electrolyte replacement, then benzodiazepine titration. Sequence matters: glucose-containing fluids should not precede thiamine.

opts.text: Reassess CIWA-Ar, vital signs, mental status every 15-30 minutes; adjust lorazepam dose accordingly; replace electrolytes (potassium, phosphorus) per protocol
opts.rat: THIRD - active titration is the core nursing role. Patient may need many doses of lorazepam over hours. Electrolyte replacement happens concurrently. Documentation of CIWA-Ar trend is critical for handoff.

opts.text: Coordinate ICU transfer with bed control, SBAR to receiving team, transport with cardiac monitoring and benzodiazepine available en route
opts.rat: FOURTH - patient needs ICU level care for severe DTs. Transport itself is high-risk - autonomic instability and possible seizure during the move. Bring lorazepam.

opts.text: Update wife on diagnosis, treatment plan, expected course, and offer her presence at bedside (when safe); arrange addiction medicine and social work consults for post-stabilization
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - family communication and transition planning. Wife's presence often calms the patient and helps with reorientation. Addiction medicine consult during admission improves long-term sobriety rates significantly.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 24 hours of ICU benzodiazepine titration, electrolyte replacement, and supportive care, classify each finding.

opts.text: CIWA-Ar score decreased from 28 to 9; oriented x3; no longer hallucinating; calm and conversational
opts.rat: Major clinical improvement. CIWA <10 generally indicates the patient can be transitioned to lower acuity care. Resolution of hallucinations and orientation are the strongest signs of DT recovery.

opts.text: BP 134/82, HR 88, no diaphoresis, mild residual tremor on outstretched hands
opts.rat: Autonomic surge resolving as GABA tone restored by benzodiazepines. Residual fine tremor is common and resolves over days to weeks.

opts.text: Magnesium replaced to 2.0 mg/dL; potassium 4.1; phosphorus 3.0; eating soft diet; tolerating PO benzodiazepine taper
opts.rat: Electrolyte normalization plus successful PO transition signals readiness to step down from ICU. PO taper schedule (typically over 5-7 days) helps prevent rebound symptoms.

opts.text: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure x 2 minutes despite lorazepam 16 mg total; brief post-ictal period followed by re-emergence of hallucinations
opts.rat: Inadequate benzodiazepine loading - need to escalate aggressively. Some severe DTs require IV phenobarbital or even propofol/dexmedetomidine when benzodiazepines fail (resistant alcohol withdrawal). Continuous EEG may be needed.

opts.text: New rapid atrial fibrillation with rate 152, BP dropping to 88/52, lactate rising to 4.6 mmol/L
opts.rat: DT-induced cardiac complication - 'holiday heart' arrhythmia plus catecholamine cardiomyopathy. Rate control, magnesium repletion, and aggressive benzodiazepine to suppress catecholamine surge. Mortality from cardiovascular collapse in untreated DTs is real.

opts.text: Patient verbalizing intent to stop drinking, asking about AA meetings and outpatient detox, accepting referral to addiction medicine
opts.rat: Ambivalence-to-readiness transition is a meaningful psychosocial outcome. The hospitalization itself may be a 'reachable moment' for change. Concrete referrals during admission dramatically improve long-term sobriety vs verbal advice alone.

opts.text: Surgical incision site clean, dry, intact; abdomen soft; bowel sounds present; pain controlled with PO acetaminophen
opts.rat: Surgical recovery proceeding normally despite the DT episode. Post-op care continues alongside withdrawal treatment - both must be addressed concurrently.

opts.text: Family at bedside, wife verbalizing understanding of the diagnosis, the role of alcohol in the post-op course, the importance of disclosure to providers in the future, and the need for outpatient support
opts.rat: Family education is critical. Many DT cases happen because alcohol use was not disclosed at admission - prevention requires destigmatizing disclosure and routine screening. Family becomes part of the recovery support system.
